%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# B.7-4 Simple Plotting

> **Section Objective:** Master MATLAB's plot command and learn to visualize data effectively.

## Concepts In This Section

- plot(t,f) paired-vector plotting
- axis labels and titles
- marker plots for discrete points
- axis equal for geometry
- semilog and polar plotting

MATLAB's plot command is the main tool for visualizing data. In this section, you will learn how to plot continuous functions, add labels and titles, plot discrete points with custom markers, and use specialized plotting functions. Plotting is essential for understanding signal behavior and verifying calculations. In this section, we'll master the core plotting commands and avoid common mistakes.

## 1. Basic Plot Command

The plot command takes two vectors and draws one against the other. If you have 100 time values in \(t\) and 100 function values in \(f\), the command \(\text{plot}(t, f)\) will draw a line connecting all 100 points in order. MATLAB connects the dots by default, creating a piecewise linear visualization of your data.


## 2. Adding Labels and Titles

Use \(\text{xlabel}\) and \(\text{ylabel}\) to label the axes. Enclose the label text in single quotes. For example, \(\text{xlabel}('t')\) labels the horizontal axis. Use \(\text{title}('My Title')\) to add a title above the plot.

```
plot(t, f);
xlabel('t');
ylabel('f(t)');
title('Sinusoid');
```

Each command adds one element to the plot. The semicolon suppresses output display.

## 3. Plotting Discrete Points with Markers

By default, \(\text{plot}\) connects points with lines. But sometimes you want to show individual data points without connecting them—for example, when plotting the roots of a polynomial on the complex plane. Add a third argument to \(\text{plot}\), a string like \('o'\), to mark each point with a circle instead of a line.

Other common markers include:
- \('x'\) — cross marker
- \('*'\) — asterisk marker
- \('+'\) — plus marker
- \('s'\) — square marker

The marker string is optional and changes the visual style from connected lines to discrete points.


## 4. The axis equal Command

When plotting on the complex plane, the horizontal and vertical scales may differ by default, making a circle appear as an ellipse. The command \(\text{axis equal}\) forces both axes to use the same scale, so a circle looks like a circle.

### WHY THIS MATTERS FOR THE EXAM

This is crucial when verifying that roots lie on the unit circle or when comparing magnitudes in different directions. Without \(\text{axis equal}\), the plot can be misleading and your visual verification will fail.

```
plot(real(w), imag(w), 'o');
axis equal;
```

## 5. Specialized Plotting Functions

MATLAB offers many specialized plotting commands for different data types:

- \(\text{semilogx}\) — logarithmic scale on the horizontal axis
- \(\text{semilogy}\) — logarithmic scale on the vertical axis
- \(\text{loglog}\) — logarithmic scales on both axes
- \(\text{image}\) — display images
- \(\text{contour}\) — 2D contour plots
- \(\text{plot3}\) — 3D lines
- \(\text{mesh}\) and \(\text{surf}\) — 3D surfaces

Logarithmic plotting is useful for data that spans many orders of magnitude. For now, focus on \(\text{plot}\), \(\text{xlabel}\), \(\text{ylabel}\), and \(\text{axis equal}\). The other commands follow similar patterns.

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- \(\text{plot}(t, f)\) draws \(f\) against \(t\), connecting points with lines by default.
- Use \(\text{xlabel}\), \(\text{ylabel}\), and \(\text{title}\) to add labels and titles to plots.
- Add a marker string like \('o'\) as a third argument to plot discrete points instead of connected lines.
- Use \(\text{axis equal}\) to ensure equal scales on both axes, preventing circles from appearing as ellipses.

*In the next section, we will explore more advanced MATLAB operations and functions.*
